data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: A Fortress In A Sea Of Geopolitical Risk
data.description:
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B)(BRK.A) made headlines several times these last few weeks, a period that coincided with the outbreak of a war in Iran that sent global markets into disarray. First, incoming CEO Greg Abel published the first post-1965 Berkshire shareholder letter written by anyone other than Warren Buffett. Later, he announced that he'd invested $15 million into Berkshire and resumed buybacks for the first time since May of 2024. These developments are material to Berkshire investors--particularly since this is a time of elevated geopolitical risk. So, let's explore them in more detail.

First, the shareholder letter.

This was a significant development because it showed how Abel was thinking immediately after he took the reins of Berkshire in the first quarter.

Berkshire’s shareholder letters are among the best-regarded literature in the world of investing, being studied and picked over by investors from all kinds of backgrounds. For more than six decades, they were written by none other than the Oracle of Omaha himself. Buffett’s letters were praised not only for their investing wisdom, but also for their clear writing and witty observations. So, Abel had some big shoes to fill when his letter came out.

As it turned out, Abel was more than up to the task. For the most part, Abel’s letter continued with Buffett’s tradition of wisdom, wit and clarity, shedding light on Berkshire’s philosophy on risk and opportunity. It was received well by investors, with many noting its honoring of Buffett’s legacy.

Next, the investment and buyback.

These moves were significant mainly because of the management confidence they signalled. Management buys are seen as bullish signals; buybacks return wealth to shareholders much like dividends do. Abel's investment and buyback indicated that he was bullish on Berkshire when he made the decisions, which were received well: Berkshire stock shot up 2.56% the day they were announced.

Unfortunately, not everything that happened this past weekend was well received by investors. To the contrary, some developments in the Middle East got investors panicking unlike anything seen since COVID.

On Saturday, February 28, the U.S. and Israel began striking Iran over its nuclear program. The targets of the strikes included Iran’s leaders, air defence systems and Navy. The military action began with Israel carrying out “decapitation strikes” against Iran’s military leadership, eliminating the country’s Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Khamenei. Shortly afterward, the U.S. started striking Iranian infrastructure.

As the action unfolded, numerous Iranian and Gulf/Israeli targets were hit. Iranian ships were sunk, and numerous buildings and missile launchers were hit with missiles. Iran hit U.S. bases throughout the Middle East; bombed hotels and airports in Gulf countries; and attacked Israel with a barrage of missiles.

Within five days of hostilities commencing, asset prices began reacting. Oil futures shot up to near-$95. Stocks entered a period of elevated volatility, with several days of heavy losses, as well as dramatic recoveries.

In the midst of all this carnage, Berkshire Hathaway stock delivered a surprisingly calm performance, rising 2.6% while the S&P whipsawed and closed last Thursday flat over a five day period.

The past week has been a period of considerable outperformance for Berkshire. And while it might be a little callous to point it out, this outperformance will likely persist as long as geopolitics remain as they are. Berkshire Hathaway has a noted tendency to outperform during bear markets, and the current geopolitical situation is basically bearish for most stocks. For this reason, I’ve been upping my Berkshire Hathaway holdings lately. I plan to continue doing so for as long as the level of geopolitical risk in the world remains elevated–which could be for several months to come.

The Core of My Thesis

Because this article mainly focuses on one specific aspect of Berkshire Hathaway–its resilience in periods of elevated risk–I should briefly go over my overall thesis on the stock, so readers can know where I’m coming from when I talk about the risk aspect.

As I wrote in my last article on Berkshire Hathaway, I rated the stock a strong buy owing to its combination of management competence and a sensible valuation. This remains the core of my thesis: the company is well run and its shares are cheap. In fact, the shares are slightly cheaper now than they were when I last wrote about Berkshire, as the stock has dipped very slightly since that time (while outperforming the S&P 500).

Nevertheless, major changes have taken place since my last article published: most notably, the initiation of a major regional conflict and Greg Abel’s first moves as CEO of Berkshire. Both of these developments merit examination, as they impact the relative value of Berkshire stock compared to other assets available on the market.

The Iran War: an Incentive to De-Risking

By far the biggest news in the world over the last two weeks has been the U.S.-Israeli led war in Iran. The conflict has exacted immense tolls both human and financial. The casualties have been estimated at hundreds on the Iranian side, dozens on the Israeli side, and seven on the American side. Likewise, several U.S. assets in the region have been devastated, with bases and embassies alike being hit. Finally, there have been reports of attacks on civilian targets in Gulf Arab countries.

Put simply, this is a true War, with a capital W, and that means an environment of elevated risk. So, it should come as no surprise that asset prices have reacted accordingly. As is often the case in wars, oil prices have shot up, having recently hit $92.50 after trading in the sixties for most of the prior year. Likewise, tanker rates have shot up and non-energy stock prices have gone down.

It’s an environment of elevated volatility. And Berkshire Hathaway is perfectly positioned to thrive in it, in three main ways:

  1. Berkshire has significant investments in oil, including large positions in Chevron (CVX) and Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

  2. The company's balance sheet is highly de-risked, with more than $370 billion worth of treasuries in its portfolio. Treasuries are not exciting assets–they are often referred to as “cash”--but they are close to risk-free. So, holding treasuries de-risks your portfolio. At the same time, treasuries do earn a return (about 4% for the 10 year right now); they do something for you, and are not equivalent to simply holding cash earning 0.01% in a checking account, or stashing physical bills under your mattress.

  3. The company’s large horde of cash and treasuries provides dry powder for doing deals should adequate opportunities become available.

So, Berkshire has considerable oil exposure, and a nice stash of treasuries that serves as both risk management and liquidity for future deals. This is precisely what you want in an environment of elevated geopolitical risk.

Abel’s First Moves

In addition to the breakout of a major conflict, the past few weeks have also seen Greg Abel’s first confirmed moves as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway. As mentioned previously, those included the shareholder letter and the initiation of a new buyback. I’d also include the company’s overall late 2025 performance because, while Buffett was still CEO then, Abel was already transitioning to his new role.

So, let's review the new developments under Abel.

First, the earnings. These slightly missed expectations, and were followed by a minor selloff in Berkshire stock. A miss is never something an investor likes to see, but if you look at the main reason for Berkshire’s Q4 miss (increased advertising spend at GEICO), it’s one that points to future growth. Short term “pain” for long term gain.

Next up, the shareholder letter. It was quite long, and included much operational detail, including specific financials from Berkshire’s operating subsidiaries that weren’t required under generally accepted accounting principles [GAAP]. Broadly, it gave the impression of an author with a lot of skin in the game and deep knowledge of the intricacies of Berkshire’s operating businesses. These are traits that investors desire in a CEO, and Abel ticks the boxes.

Last but not least we have Abel’s $15 million stock purchase and re-initiation of buybacks. The stock purchase showed confidence in Berkshire from a key insider. The buyback is likewise a vote of confidence in Berkshire by its management. Together, these signals indicate that Berkshire’s management think that the company will continue being profitable and will probably grow going forward.

What’s Berkshire Really Worth?

So far, I discussed some recent news items that I considered bullish for Berkshire. Namely, the increase in geopolitical risk across the entire market; the increase in oil prices; and Greg Abel’s first moves as CEO. I found all of these factors to be bullish, the first two in the short term and the third in the long term. Based on these factors, I think Berkshire Hathaway has a solid shot at delivering positive earnings growth, maybe somewhere between 5% and 10% per year over the next five years.

In the last 12 months, Berkshire Hathaway did $44.5 billion in operating earnings, or $20.62 on a per class-B share basis. The stock cost $497 as of market close on Monday. That gives us a P/E ratio of 24 for Berkshire. By another estimate (GAAP earnings), the stock has a 16 P/E ratio. These P/E ratios translate to earnings yields between 4.16% and 6.25%. Both of these earnings yields are higher than the 10 year treasury yield, the second one significantly so. The second earnings yield I calculated doesn’t require any growth for Berkshire to be worth the investment; the first requires a little. As I wrote above, Berkshire should actually do 5% to 10% annualized earnings growth going forward. So, no matter how you calculate Berkshire’s earnings, its shares are worth more than treasuries today.

Risks

Despite its fortress-like qualities, Berkshire is not risk-free. To the contrary, those invested in the stock assume real risks, including:

  • The Insurance Underwriting Cycle. The Property & Casualty [P&C] insurance industry is characterized by 'boom and bust' cycles brought on by competitors entering the market chasing profits, followed by busts where the number of competitors drives margins down. Profitability has been high across the U.S. P&C sector for several years. So, it's fairly likely that a cyclical downturn is on the horizon. If so, Berkshire's margins will likely take a hit.
  • Portfolio concentration. Berkshire Hathaway's portfolio is relatively concentrated, with the top 10 holdings making up 88.3% of the total by this author's calculations. This concentration puts Berkshire at risk of underperformance in a scenario where one of the top stocks experiences performance issues.
  • Regulatory risk. Several of Berkshire Hathaway's biggest companies (GEICO, General Re, BNSF) are in highly regulated sectors. Shifts in insurance and railroad regulations could impose new costs on Berkshire.

The risks above are real and worth thinking about. For my money, though, the potential return here is appealing given the level of risk.

The Bottom Line

The bottom line on Berkshire Hathaway stock is that it’s a fortress of a company at a time of elevated geopolitical risk. At a time of global conflict, few things are likely to perform better than a company with substantial investments in oil, and excellent risk management practices. These factors make the case for an investment in Berkshire Hathaway stock today. The fact that new CEO Greg Abel seems to be doing a great job is just icing on the cake.

data.title: Outlook For Interest Rates As Conflict Clouds Inflation Picture
data.description:
Investors had been counting on rate cuts from the Fed in 2026, but Alexandra Gorewicz, VP and Director for Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management, tells MoneyTalk the outlook is less certain as the Iran conflict pushes oil prices higher.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: With soaring energy prices, we have been concerned about inflation. US consumer prices coming in largely as expected. But of course, we got inflation still sitting above the Fed's target, gas price concerns. Are interest rate cuts still on the table for 2026? Joining us now to discuss this is Alex Gorewicz, VP and Director for Active Fixed Income Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management.

We'll clear the air to start with. These are backwards-- backward-looking numbers now from CPI. Does it matter as much what we got today, or the situation that we're in and moving through?

Alexandra Gorewicz: Certainly, the situation that we're in and moving through in the Middle East matters a lot more for interest rates, for what you think central banks are going to do. But today's CPI number came in line with expectations. Call it 0.2% to 0.3% for headline CPI or core CPI, that's excluding food and energy. And then year on year, about 2.4%, 2.5%. Because those numbers came in line with the expectations, underlying details were all pointing to pretty benign inflation. On a backward-looking basis, bond market didn't react to that print.

Greg Bonnell: Didn't react to the prints. Let's talk about the situation we're in right now. It is fluid. We have seen-- I mean, just in the past couple of days-- I know you've been on calls with your colleagues. Hussein Allidina was in our studio to start the week. Oil rocketing to almost $120, falling below $80 right now, approaching $90. What do we do with those kind of fluctuations where we're trying to figure out where we're headed in terms of cost of living?

Alexandra Gorewicz: So, to me, the fluctuations in energy prices in capital markets worry me a lot less than every passing day hearing about more disruptions. And Hussein and his commodity team are instrumental to us getting that detailed analysis on what is happening day over day. Because it is important to remember that it's not just about energy-- there are a lot of other commodities that flow through that region.

And if I take more even of a-- we'll call it a macro view-- beyond just commodities, any type of supply-related disruption, as we've seen not that long ago, only a few years ago, could result in more sustained inflation globally. And then it will matter from country to country.

Greg Bonnell: What is the Fed supposed to do with all of this? I mean, the markets are already pushing out the idea that you're going to get a cut. Maybe it's in September. Some participants even throwing it out there-- maybe not at all. How do we start thinking about where the Fed can move?

Alexandra Gorewicz: So, if we look at right now-- and, again, this is not related to the CPI print-- there was no repricing of Fed expectations on that CPI print. If we look at the broader issue with Iran, and since the start of the war-- if I could even call it that-- we've seen every central bank get repriced hawkishly. In other words, if there were rate cuts, now there are rate hikes priced in for 2026.

The Fed is the only one, especially among developed markets, that is still pricing rate cuts, but just one less than we were pricing at the end of February. So, right now, I think the bond market has one and a half rate cuts to the end of the year. Whereas before, it was closer to two and a half and three.

Greg Bonnell: The thing about the Fed, as well, is that we're going to get a new chair, right? This is inevitable. It has been someone, presumably, the US president has picked thinking that they would be a little more aligned with the White House's vision of where rates should be. Where does that start to come into play? Is this a conflict at all? Or do you figure Mr. Warsh will go with the data, just like any central banker would?

Alexandra Gorewicz: I think the immediate reaction-- when it was announced that he would be the nominee, the immediate reaction from the bond market was that we'd have more of, we'll call it, a reasoned approach to monetary policy, in that there must still be economic justification for the decisions the Fed will be making. And I think investors are probably still expecting that.

The challenge now is-- and we haven't heard Warsh speak. So this is-- we're looking back a little bit in terms of--

Greg Bonnell: You're looking at his history, trying to figure out who he's going to be in the big chair?

Alexandra Gorewicz: Correct. So we need to keep that in mind, too. But if we look back at a lot of his reasoning or argumentation, I think he relies a lot on the fact that the AI productivity gain will more than offset any kind of inflationary impact. But this was pre-Iran War.

Now, with every passing day, like I mentioned, is we have to start worrying about supply disruptions globally and across sectors, not just commodity-specific. Now we need to consider how the potential for more sustained inflation will be weighed in his mind against the AI productivity boom. Too early to tell. And here, I would say it's hard to paint every single country with one brush stroke.

So what do I mean? If you're an energy-insecure country, basically you import the vast majority of your energy needs or you have an energy trade deficit, like, say, Europe and certainly Asia, you've seen a much more pronounced hawkish repricing of those central banks. In other words, there is expected to be more sustained inflation beyond just a one or, we'll call it, a couple of quarters of energy price shocks.

In North America-- US included-- we're not net energy importers. Quite the opposite. And especially Canada, a significant exporter. And there, the situation is a little bit more nuanced. The bond market is saying, "Yeah, we're going to reprice Bank of Canada and Fed a little bit more hawkish." But not nearly as much as other countries that are going to be much more impacted by this energy shock.

Greg Bonnell: When we have this much uncertainty and this much risk-- I'm taking a look at the US 10-year. It's moved higher, the yield's higher. It's a little above 4.2% right now. But is the bond market right now behaving in a way you think it would behave with this much risk on the table?

Alexandra Gorewicz: Certainly not in North America. And the reason I say this is that North America-- Canada and the US-- have seen inflation expectations-- which is one of the key components of how interest rates move. They've seen those inflation expectations quite anchored, especially at the long end of the curve. It speaks to investors expecting this not to be a durable inflation shock.

That's not what other markets are saying. In particular, the ones that have large energy trade deficits. But someone's going to be right here, right? And the question is who? So when we look in North America, a large part of the repricing has not come through inflation expectations. It's come through real yields. In other words, there could be some net benefits longer term for our economies, which is probably why the rise in yields has been a lot more contained than in other markets. But again, every passing day, I worry that that shoe's going to drop on inflation here at home too.

Greg Bonnell: What does a fixed-income investor do with all that information? What can we do at this point?

Alexandra Gorewicz: Yeah. So, the reality is to try to create some sense of calm within what's happening in bond land. It's really difficult because a lot of people draw parallels to Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the spike in energy prices at that time.

I think it's important for bond investors to remember one critical difference between now and then. Interest rates are higher. When interest rates are higher, the coupons that you're getting, the cash flows you're generating from your bond investments are substantially higher. Those provide a cushion for this kind of volatility that we're seeing. That's extremely important.

The second thing-- and that is probably less focused on, and it's less about bond investments and it's more about, we'll call it, asset allocation, how to think about bonds relative to equities-- is how the volatility in bond land is behaving today versus equities, and compare that to 2022. In 2022, with Russia's invasion of Ukraine, both equity and bond volatility went higher. But bond volatility went a lot higher. It was very clear to markets that that was a bigger deal for bonds than it was for stocks, although both of them had very bad years.

That's not the case this time around. This time around, you're seeing a small increase in bond market volatility, especially in North America, but a much more pronounced one in equities. To me, this is saying that the longer this goes on, the more the shoe is likely to drop in risk assets than bond land. And again, that could be a function of just higher income that is providing more of a cushion.

Original Post

data.title: Forget Oil: Iran War Could Eventually Trigger AI Recession
data.description:
Thesis Summary

The market is quite understandably hyper-focused on oil right now, as escalation in Iran continues to limit supply.

But with the war potentially dragging on longer than expected, other bottlenecks are beginning to appear.

It's not just oil that travels through the Strait of Hormuz, and also not just oil that is produced in the Middle East.

If these disruptions persist, we could see other markets affected beyond just oil.

The two most pressing examples of this, right now, are fertilizers and helium.

Fertilizer prices have skyrocketed and could become a real problem for inflation and consumers.

But perhaps even worse, a lack of helium could disrupt the AI supply chain and even trigger an AI recession.

Oil Is The Obvious Problem

Around 20% of global oil and LNG supply travels through the Strait of Hormuz, which is why it's bad news for everyone, not just for the Middle East, when supply is disrupted.

As I've discussed before, countries like South Korea are entirely reliant on energy exports, and so are many other nations.

With the Strait of Hormuz still blocked, at least partially, crude has rallied toward $120 per barrel.

And even if the situation normalizes eventually, the threat of closure will remain an overhang for some time

According to estimates from the IMF, a sustained 10% increase in energy prices can add roughly 0.4% to global inflation while also shaving off 0.2% off global growth.

Take this to the extreme, and you get stagflation.

But while everyone is focusing on oil, there are also other key commodities that investors should be aware of.

The Fertilizer Shock (And Opportunity)

There's another supply chain that has been disrupted, and that's fertilizer.

The Gulf region accounts for a massive share of global fertilizer trade.

Almost half of global traded urea, the most widely used nitrogen fertilizer, comes from countries in this area.

On top of that, around 30% of global ammonia exports and about half of global sulfur trade also come from this region.

The war in Iran isn't just disrupting shipping in the Strait of Hormuz; it's disrupting production in a lot of neighboring countries.

Most notably, Qatar's Ras Laffan industrial complex, one of the world's largest LNG and fertilizer hubs, has had to temporarily halt operations following Iranian strikes.

As a result of this, the price of fertilizers has skyrocketed.

Urea prices have surged between 19% and more than 70%, depending on the benchmark, while some import markets saw fertilizer prices jump around 30% in a single week.

And this is happening at the worst possible time, just as half the world enters spring seasons.

This matters to investors for two reasons.

Firstly, this could also lead to a rise in inflation and pressure on consumers.

And secondly, because fertilizer stocks have exploded higher, opening up another investing opportunity in this turbulent market.

Helium: The New AI Bottleneck

Few investors associate helium with semiconductors or geopolitics, but I guess you learn something new every day.

Apparently, helium is one of the most critical industrial gases used in semiconductor manufacturing.

It is used in wafer fabrication as well as leak detection and cooling systems throughout the AI supply chain.

Without helium, semiconductor manufacturing becomes significantly more difficult and expensive.

Qatar, which we mentioned above, accounts for roughly ⅓ of the global helium supply, and of course, much of that production is shipped through the Strait of Hormuz.

The AI boom is fundamentally a semiconductor boom.

The massive investments in data centers are indirectly an investment in GPUs, AI accelerators, and other forms of hardware and software.

Semiconductor supply chains are already stretched, and now we can add helium to this mix.

Could War Cause An AI Recession?

The AI narrative is about developing a technology that will make us much more productive, someday.

But the AI impulse to the economy we are seeing today is ultimately a CAPEX story.

Hyperscalers are spending hundreds of billions of dollars building data centers, AI infrastructure, and next-generation chips.

There are literally trillions of dollars on the line, and a disruption to the buildout could halt this process.

The Iran war could halt this AI expansion in numerous ways.

High energy prices could lead to higher inflation and tighter monetary conditions.

But more immediately, supply bottlenecks, like a shortage in helium, could spark delays in the CAPEX rollout, trigger write-downs, and eventually reverse the whole CAPEX cycle.

As the enormous investment pipeline currently flowing into AI infrastructure could begin to slow, this would be felt in the economy, market expectations, and earnings.

Maybe not the end of AI, but at the very least an AI recession.

It's Still Early To Tell

Although some investors are already panicking, this may be premature.

For example, SK hynix said in a recent statement that they had diverse suppliers and enough inventory for some time.

On top of that, the most recent export data from South Korea doesn't show a slowdown in semiconductor sales.

All in all, while this may be a threat, it will only become a problem if we see this war extend for months, rather than weeks.

Final Thoughts

The war in Iran is disrupting markets, and indeed, based on recent developments, it seems like it could stretch out for longer. Equities may have a hard time in the next few months, and we could see some bottlenecks appear, but it's still early to call for an AI recession.

Ultimately, I think this could be a good change to add exposure as the correction unfolds.

data.title: Uber And Zoox: The Partnership Proves AVs An Opportunity Not A Threat (Upgrade)
data.description:
Uber Technologies (UBER) has become a ubiquitous ride-hailing and delivery behemoth internationally, complete with its own verb: to “uber.” It has one of the most recognizable brands, and its growth across various metrics over the past five years has been nothing short of phenomenal.

Data above is on a TTM basis, and we can see that revenue has jumped from around $10 billion in 2021 to over $50 billion in the most recent quarter (again, on a TTM basis). Revenue growth was at 18.3% last year (20% when comparing Q4 2025 with Q4 2024), which for a company of its size is fantastic. Similarly, TTM FCF and operating income were both negative up until Q2 2022 and Q3 2023, respectively, but are now firmly in the positive and growing fast. FCF growth year-over-year to end 2025 was 41.6%, while operating income nearly doubled (98.8%) year-over-year. Gross bookings, its most important growth metric to assess consumer demand, grew 22% when comparing the 4th quarters of 2024 and 2025. This metric seems to be accelerating, which, again, for a company of its size, is phenomenal. The demand for Uber’s various offerings remains strong.

I also included shareholders’ equity in the first image (above) to highlight the company’s improving financial health. Uber has always had a large amount of debt on its books, which has spooked some investors/analysts, but the steadily growing total value of their assets, alongside their falling debt/equity ratio (now at a comfortable 0.42), indicates a stable business that has lots of room to invest in growth. Indeed, with almost $10 billion generated in FCF in the TTM period, this almost covers their total long-term debt ($10.5 billion) and more than covers the debt due in the next 5 years (around $5.6 billion according to their latest 10-K annual report). They have minimal current debt, due in the next 12 months. This affords the company an enormous reserve of liquidity to invest in antecedent businesses and to generate partnerships with businesses that could even be classed as competitors.

In this article, I’d like to highlight some of these new developments, since my last coverage of Uber in October 2025. In that write-up, I discussed especially their exciting developments in on-demand product delivery, and since then the company has reported two quarters of positive earnings and announced several dozen news pieces in relation to business expansions into new and exciting areas. In this article, in particular, I’d like to highlight its new and exciting partnership with Zoox, an Amazon (AMZN) subsidiary, in the broader context of Uber’s positioning in the nascent market for autonomous vehicle (AV) ride-hailing (robo-taxis), which has been considered an existential threat to Uber’s business for several years now. I believe it is a key reason for the company’s low valuation relative to other tech peers, but for me it represents a key opportunity for investors given the market’s overestimation of this risk and/or the mispricing of its impact on Uber’s business. Thus, for me, Uber as an investment continues to offer an asymmetrical risk/reward profile, as I’ll outline below.

I have covered Uber on three occasions and have a consistent buy rating on the company, as I believe in its long-term future prosperity. Shares are modestly up since my first 2 articles back in November 2024 (here and here), but are down around 20% since my last bullish call on the company, which has led me to ask, is my investment thesis broken, or do shares now represent an even better bargain than before?

As an Uber driver myself, I figured I could offer a different perspective to most analysts, from the combined perspective of customer, driver, and investor. I have completed over 6,000 rides as an Uber driver and maintain, I think, a good understanding of the business’ potential to leverage its enormous network of drivers and loyal customers alongside its diverse technology stack into new product verticals. I remain as bullish as ever and view the current drawdown as an opportunity to purchase more shares at a discount.

Recent Developments

In the past 5 months—since my last coverage—Uber has made over a dozen different announcements regarding partnerships. Together, they highlight the company’s renewed focus on their mission ‘to power movement from point A to point ’B’—to become, in a sense, the go-to app for the short- and medium-distance movement/delivery of people and products.

In mobility—its main business, representing 57% of its 2025 revenue—Uber recently secured a win to have its services potentially offered for the first time in Israel. A change in government policy there, enabling cheaper transportation options for passengers, has invited Uber and Lyft (LYFT) access to a country that had so far proven an impenetrable market. What news pieces like this do is continually validate the value and benefit that cities/countries see in Uber. Despite the noise about regulatory headwinds, Uber continues to see progress in new markets, because what it offers is flexibility, competitive pricing, and convenience for customers that traditional taxi services do not. This also extends to non-traditional business verticals. For example, Uber recently announced a partnership with Vail Resorts (MTN) to offer a product called “Uber Ski,” which allows users to book ski resort transportation in advance but also purchase lift tickets. In mobility, Uber recognized revenue growth of 18.3% in 2025.

In delivery—which represented 33.2% of Uber’s 2025 revenue—Uber continues to see strong growth (25.4% year over year). In food delivery, they have expanded their offerings with Kroger (KR). Customers can now shop for groceries at any one of Kroger’s ~2,700 stores using the Uber app, adding the convenience of on-demand and same-day delivery. Uber also announced its plans to purchase the parking app SpotHero, which I view as a strategic investment to enable its premium Uber One members an extra feature: parking benefits at events, venues, and airports.

Internationally, Uber recently announced plans to acquire Getir’s delivery portfolio in Turkey for $335 million, bringing Getir and Trendyol Go delivery under the Uber umbrella. Uber will also invest $100 million for a 15% stake in Getir’s grocery, retail, and water delivery business, with tentative plans to acquire the remainder of this business in the next few years.

These developments add to the strong growth Uber recently announced (in the Q3 2025 call) with regard to new merchant wins, as seen below.

Uber is building rapidly in this area, with merchants equally keen to partner up with Uber to drive growth in their own businesses. Such a mutually beneficial relationship that drives customer penetration and revenue growth, together, represents a very powerful tool for the company to leverage, and on balance I believe it has done very well in establishing Uber Eats as a dominant player in this business segment.

In payments, Uber has partnered with Global Payments (GPN)—to become their ‘preferred delivery partner’—and ’renewed their 13-year relationship with Adyen (ADYEY) in relation to payment solutions; the latter aims to create Uber kiosks in airports, hotels, and venues globally. They also deployed Cart Assistant in their food delivery vertical, which adds an AI capability that, in the words of Grocery Dive:

‘[A]llows shoppers to build full grocery baskets using simple text or image prompts, rather than having to scroll through digital aisles. Consumers can upload photos of handwritten meal plans or screenshots of recipes, and Cart Assistant will generate a shopping list using the information’.

This new feature will enable it to compete more successfully with Instacart (CART), which presently uses a third-party feature to enable the creation of a digital grocery list that can be converted into an Instacart order.

Uber’s other main segment, freight, recognized $5.1 billion in revenue, thereby flat year-over-year growth, representing approximately 9.8% of total revenue.

AV Deployment and Partnership News

For investors, it is likely the news related to AVs that represents the most exciting development of late. Beginning with the expanded partnership between Uber and WeRide (WRD), both companies have collaborated to deploy robo-taxis in Dubai (from December 2025) and Abu Dhabi (from February 2026). The launch in Abu Dhabi last month began by offering rides covering approximately 70% of the city area, in partnership with its Integrated Transport Centre. Uber developed a new “autonomous” category through their app in these locations and announced plans to expand this partnership to 1,200 robo-taxis across the entire Middle East region by 2027. This would also incorporate Riyadh alongside Dubai and Abu Dhabi, and add 12 further cities in the Middle East region by 2030. Uber also recently announced a partnership with Baidu (BIDU) in Dubai, which has plans to have 25% of all transportation trips be autonomous by 2030. This partnership also comes off the back of a planned expansion into London this year.

The Middle East is widely considered the best major market to deploy new transportation technologies, including autonomous driving/robo-taxi services on its city streets alongside EVTOL with partnerships established with Joby (JOBY) and Archer (ACHR). Indeed, a few weeks ago, Joby and Uber announced “Uber Air,” offering passengers in Dubai the opportunity to book short trips in Joby’s state-of-the-art 4-passenger flying vehicles through the Uber app. The feature for passengers also includes an Uber Black ride to and from the pick-up and drop-off locations (the relevant heliports).

Uber’s penetration in the Middle East is considered important strategically in the medium-to-long term. While revenue from these operations is unlikely to really move the needle for the company for the time being, what it provides them is exposure to developing markets, cross-selling opportunities (driving customers to its app for regular mobility and food/package delivery), and conceptual validation of its technology and industry capabilities across different verticals. This last one may prove the most important benefit of the investment in this region. Uber’s name will become synonymous, if it hasn’t already, with advanced mobility technologies, such that any new market entrant must consider partnering with or potentially competing against it, and any new government transport authority seeking to expand transportation offerings in this area must consider Uber as a potential partner. The more they prove themselves as dominant in this space, the more their leadership, if not ubiquitous presence or even dominance, becomes self-perpetuating.

Uber also expanded its robotaxi deployment efforts in the US. Alongside agreeing to invest $100 million to develop new AV charging stations across numerous American cities, Uber recently announced expansion into Dallas with Avride. This builds off the partnership established with Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo, offering autonomous rides in Phoenix, primarily through the Waymo app, and also in Austin and Atlanta, which has shown strong growth in both cities, latterly, as seen below.

Uber also recently announced expansion plans into Tokyo, partnering with Nissan (NSANY) and Wayve, the Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA) backed AV start-up. Also in the area of autonomous driving, Uber developed a partnership with Starship Technologies to deploy sidewalk delivery robots to the UK and other parts of Europe this year. Starship has already performed more than 9 million deliveries across 7 countries, using level-4 autonomy (no human intervention) for last-mile delivery services. They plan to roll this out in the US in 2027.

Uber’s Position within the Nascent AV Ride-hailing Market

In relation to their ongoing and quite aggressive investments in AV technology and deployment, Uber remarked in their latest 10-K annual report on both its potential and threat:

‘We have invested, and we may continue to invest, substantial amounts in companies with whom we partner to offer autonomous vehicle technologies on our platform. For example, our investments in and commercial partnerships with various autonomous mobility and/or autonomous delivery companies currently support the development and deployment of autonomous vehicle technology capable of operation in the United States and globally. We believe that autonomous vehicle technologies may have the ability to meaningfully impact the industries in which we compete and that autonomous vehicles present substantial opportunities. Several companies, including Waymo, Tesla, and Zoox (a subsidiary of Amazon), are developing autonomous vehicle technologies in the United States, either alone or through collaborations with car manufacturers, as are similar companies globally, and we expect that they will use such technology to further compete with us in the mobility, delivery, or logistics industries.’

After highlighting Waymo’s commercialized ride-hailing fleet of AVs, it goes on to note that the company could face competitive pressures if they are too slow to develop this technology:

‘In the event that our competitors bring autonomous vehicles to market before we are able to offer autonomous vehicles on our platform, deploy their autonomous vehicles on ridesharing, delivery or logistics platforms other than ours, or their technology is or is perceived to be superior to the technology of parties with which we partner to offer autonomous vehicles on our platform, or their infrastructure or operational expertise in support of autonomous vehicles is or is perceived to be superior or more efficient or profitable than offerings on our platform, they may be able to leverage such technology to compete more effectively with us, which would adversely impact our financial performance and our prospects.’

Given these comments, we might consider the main threats to Uber in this space as them being potentially too slow to market or having some kind of technological or market-based inferiority. Let’s dig into these threats.

Regarding the first threat—being too slow or left behind—I would point to the fact that they have now established over 20 relationships/partnerships with AV companies globally.

Rather than being overtaken or left out of this burgeoning but still very much nascent industry development, I believe this positions them at the very forefront of its growth. My confidence in Uber’s ability to take share in the AV space is based on my own personal understanding—as an Uber driver—of how carefully they manage to grow both sides of the market, the passengers and the drivers/vehicles. Straddling both of these areas, they claim in their latest earnings presentation to have superiority in relation to other robo-taxi providers in terms of efficiency and time.

Using the example of Austin, where they operate both human drivers and AVs, they have shown how the demand for both is complementary in terms of providing the best possible service for customers. They effectively use AVs to supplement the demand for human drivers, but anticipate possibly at some point this being reversed, where human drivers provide the “back-up” needed to service customer demand during busy periods of the day/night. When Uber has talked previously, especially in the Q3 2025 earnings call, about a “hybrid future” (one of the company’s 6 core pillars of growth going forward), this is what they mean—hybrid: human and autonomous, operating side-by-side.

Any company that only offers AVs—like Tesla (TSLA) and Waymo, presently – will have to compete against Uber’s more flexible offerings, thereby returning lower margins as a business. Uber’s two-pronged approach affords it incredible leverage in the market. It states:

‘An AV only competitor must hold significant underutilized supply to match Uber’s reliability and prices or deliver a worse consumer experience and leave significant demand on the table.’

In time, as customers vote with their feet based on quality and flexibility of service, Uber stands to benefit as this industry grows. Moreover, no company can compete with Uber’s broad geographical coverage, shown below in relation to the US market.

To think that Waymo's or Tesla’s robotaxis could eventually grow to more than 8,000 US markets and cover 95% of the nation’s population is, to me, pie in the sky, and an investment based solely on this premise would be highly speculative. They would not have the time or investment capabilities (deployable cash) to come close. As I see it, no AV company can compete with Uber’s human driver network, and only Lyft has the opportunity to compete with Uber in terms of establishing AV partners. However, the latter has significantly less capital to invest and cannot possibly grow as fast or develop as many partnerships through investments. With similar debt/equity ratios, Uber’s superiority in its cash holdings and FCF generation highlight its competitive advantage. With its stronger cash position, they can invest/scale into business much more rapidly.

It is in this context of assessing their competitive moat that I view the latest partnership with Zoox as particularly exciting, if not also validating of my thesis. Earlier this week, Uber and Zoox announced a strategic partnership to deploy the Zoox robotaxis on the Uber platform. Details of the partnership center on the initial rollout in Las Vegas in 2026, where a limited service will be offered—initially within geofenced zones and event-driven—but expanding to Los Angeles next year. Remember that Zoox was listed as a key competitor to Uber in their latest 10-K annual report, and this move has effectively crossed them off the list, as now they are partners.

Zoox robo-taxis are purpose-built, symmetrical, and offer bi-directional travel, designed for comfort and convenience, with carriage-style seating. As they announced in their own press release, Zoox has already established itself in Las Vegas and San Francisco, completing one million miles involving over 300,000 passengers, and has imminent plans to be rolled out in Austin and Miami. The Zoox testing fleet is currently deployed in ten markets, recently adding Dallas and Phoenix, which offers Uber opportunities over the coming years to expand in these markets.

While I am not in a position to speculate as to whether Zoox will beat out its competitors like Waymo, from Uber’s perspective, what it demonstrates is that the ride-hailing juggernaut is the partner of choice for these early-stage AV start-ups. From the perspectives of these much smaller companies, it would carry great weight to be “backed by Uber” or “partnered with Uber”. Again, this adds credibility to Uber’s positioning at the forefront of this burgeoning industry, and allows it access to the best technology in this space. Moreover, what this partnership highlights is Uber’s capacity to fend off its competitors by partnering with them to create a mutually beneficial rather than antagonistic relationship. The popular saying is, If you can beat ‘em, join em’. Rather, in Uber’s case, if you don’t want to fight ‘em, partner with ‘em. In this particular case too, it brings a powerful partner (Amazon) further on side. Much like these AV companies view partnering with Uber as a powerful form of validation, no doubt Uber’s new partnership with big tech, Amazon, and—as previously announced—Nvidia, creates a strong set of allies among dominant tech industry peers. Specifically, the relationship with Nvidia forms the foundation for Uber’s transition into AVs, as they discussed in the Q3 2025 earnings call.

Uber CEO Dara Khosrowshahi remarked of the relationship:

‘In terms of NVIDIA, we are very, very excited about the partnership there. NVIDIA is creating, with Hyperion, like a reference architecture for L4-ready autonomous vehicles that they are going to make available to any OEM out there. And if you kind of step back and you think about the strategy, a future 10 years from now where every single new car sold is not only L3-ready if it's a personal car, but it's also L4-ready. If you want to contribute that car to a ridesharing platform like ours, or fleets might buy those cars as well, that is a very bright future for the world because it will make the world safer in terms of these autonomous vehicles being super safe, not getting distracted in terms of driving, but it's also very good for our ecosystem in that we will have a ready kind of supply of cars on our platform as well. And we are very early, but I think that we're quite confident in demonstrating that cars, L4 cars that are on our platform, can drive higher revenue per car per day than cars that aren't on our platform. So we think that the NVIDIA strategy and our strategy are very much aligned.’

With more and more pieces of the puzzle coming together—including AV technology, driver network, customer demand, platform applications, regulatory approvals—I view Uber’s latest partnership with Zoox as an important step towards validating their technology, network, and operational expertise—the key components of their moat as I see it—for the future they foresee as being dominated by AVs on the ground and EVTOLs in the sky.

Uber’s Business Moat

How do these latest developments enhance the company’s moat? Stemming from Uber’s latest 10-K annual report, I offer up what I feel are the company’s key competitive advantages:

  • Network effects: Uber has a massive, extensive network of customers, drivers, merchants, shippers, and carriers, and as this network grows, data collection and cross-selling opportunities improve their collective values. They form the company’s main “flywheel,” which is predicated upon the company balancing supply and demand through having the levers to deliver driver and customer incentives. Uber currently operates in over 15,000 cities around the world, which makes it the most desirable and important partner for any business—like AV, food delivery, or freight logistics companies—seeking to penetrate these individual markets.
  • Technology: Uber’s purpose-built technology covers logistics, marketplace, routing, and payments. This drives efficiencies, and, as more and more data is collected during each trip, the company can leverage this for incremental improvements that then further drive customer traction and afford it opportunities to launch new business verticals or improve existing ones. They can attain more feedback from customers and drivers than other platforms, constantly improving and modifying the technology, especially in terms of efficiency and safety, faster than their competition.
  • Operational excellence: Uber’s deep penetration within cities around the world affords it opportunities to scale more quickly than their competitors in terms of launching new product lines or customer offerings, as well as supporting the customers, drivers, merchants, shippers, and carriers on their platform. They could leverage the relationships they have already established with regulators to pursue new lines of business faster than their competitors.

When combined with the company’s strong, globally recognized brand, these competitive advantages enable Uber to attract new users and transition existing users to multiple platforms, deepening their engagement. As an Uber driver, I am afforded numerous opportunities to expand my services into other areas, for example, by extending my reach into new types of delivery (food, packages, etc.). In the latest annual report, Uber provided data to show that in Q4 2025, ‘approximately 58% of first-time Delivery consumers were new to our platform,’ and in that same period, ‘consumers who used both Mobility and Delivery generated over three times the gross bookings as compared to consumers who used a single offering in countries where both Mobility and Delivery were offered.’ Uber’s management suggests that only 1 in 5 eligible customers are active on a month-to-month basis across both of their businesses (mobility and delivery). This means there is a decent runway of potential growth ahead and plenty of opportunity to leverage the network effects Uber enjoys as a competitive advantage.

All of this is to say that Uber has been very aggressive in its investments into its own platform, and in the support of building partnerships to support its growth objectives, especially in AVs but also, of course, in delivery and other verticals. In Q3 2025, the company announced 6 key strategic objectives that it is focusing on, with ‘Building a Hybrid Future’—its ’ #2 strategic focus—representing an important area where AVs will drive growth going forward.

The extent to which the company has been successful at deploying their investments into areas that benefit the business can be seen clearly when examining its ROIC figures over the last few years, as seen below.

As allocators of capital, Uber’s management has proved exceptionally resourceful, and I see no reason why their latest investments in AVs—including their promising partnership with Zoox—won't also yield fruit in time. It is evident that Uber has a long-term vision, and their efforts in AV deployment and partnerships should pay themselves back as Uber becomes the key tech partner for companies seeking to penetrate the markets that Uber already dominates.

Should you buy shares then? Does the valuation make sense? Let’s assess this to determine the present risk-reward profile for potential investors right now.

Valuation Metrics, Risks, and Concluding Thoughts

Uber currently trades for less than 16x 2025 earnings, but this figure is skewed as a result of some non-operating income losses in the last quarter, notably a $1.6 billion pre-tax headwind from revaluations of equity investments. While its operating income saw strong growth, its net income showed a massive drop as a consequence. Therefore, the non-GAAP PE ratio of 26.6 (which discounts this figure) and forward non-GAAP PE ratio (of 22.7) offer a better indication of valuation. On this basis, Uber is trading at a historical low. Its non-GAAP PEG ratio—factoring in next year’s growth rate—is 0.91, which puts it firmly in bargain territory.

The risks to my investment thesis include the possibility that Uber might not lead the AV movement and, in fact, may be left behind by an AV-based upstart stealing market share and pushing higher margins in the process. There is also a risk that some of Uber’s investments in AVs will not bear fruit. In fact, I am convinced this will happen, but by partnering with so many, they are mitigating the risk by betting on multiple horses in the same race. This is a smart move. There will likely be only a small number of players that truly come to dominate in the AV space, but as of right now there are dozens of competitors edging forward, and we don’t know who will be leading in 5 years time. Uber is teaming up with many of them, to their mutual benefit, as a way to hedge their bets.

In my opinion, the story with Uber stock right now is a classic case of the market pricing in a medium-/long-term revenue or earnings decline that is unlikely to materialize due to a misunderstanding of how AVs are less a threat and more an opportunity for Uber. I remain convinced that Uber will come out of this AV transition as the front of the pack—leading the charge for robo-taxi deployment—rather than a has-been, once popular ridesharing app company, as the market seems to currently price it as. The risks of it being left behind are overblown in my opinion, and that is why I remain as bullish as ever.

While I might see my own personal income decline as a part-time Uber driver if/when AVs get deployed in Vancouver and begin replacing me (which I still have my doubts about, in any event), I’ll be glad to have bought shares in the company leading the way. Further, their recognition of a likely hybrid future, where both humans and AVs serve customer demand and have a place in the industry, side-by-side, seems a much more realistic outcome than a full AV takeover. Remember, eBay did not fully replace shopping malls, the Kindle did not destroy the market for books, and take-out food did not fully replace restaurant dine-in. There are numerous analogies I could use here, but you get my point. Believe it or not, people seem to genuinely enjoy talking to a human being when they are taking an Uber, as I can personally attest to!

As discussed above, Uber’s deep penetration in the industry, strong driver and passenger network, technological prowess, and operational excellence position them as the most likely long-term beneficiary of a move into AVs.

With the stock now down more than 25% from its all-time highs (around $100, back in the fall of 2025), I view the current drawdown as an incredible opportunity to pick up shares in this industry-leading business, poised for a bright future. Due to its undemanding valuation at present, I am therefore upgrading Uber shares to a strong buy upon my belief in its capacity for long-term industry leadership and disruption.

data.title: BlackRock National Municipal Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 12/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 1.27% (Institutional shares)(MANLX) and 1.21% (Investor A shares, without sales charge)(MDNLX) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • High-quality investment grade holdings in the “belly” of the yield curve (maturities in a range of 12-20 years) drove performance, while high yield positions were the largest detractor.
  • The fund had an overweight allocation to securities maturing in 12-20 years. Holdings were predominantly concentrated in high-quality positions in the tax-backed, utilities, and transportation sectors. The fund had an overweight exposure to prepaid gas debt, while it maintained a notable, albeit declining, allocation to shorter-call bonds to enhance dividend yield.

Contributors

Positions in securities maturing in 12-20 years benefited performance due to yield curve steepness, which contributed to marginal income, or carry, and price appreciation. High-quality positions in the tax-backed, utilities, and transportation sectors made solid gains. In the transportation sector, airport spreads continued to tighten, while within that space, alternative minimum tax (AMT) bonds performed well as spreads compressed versus non-AMT securities. Health care holdings focused in put bonds (bonds that give the holder the right to sell a security back to the obligor on a specified date) maturing in less than 10 years saw spread tightening.

Detractors

High yield and below investment grade holdings detracted, mainly projects in the transportation and charter school sectors. Despite attractive income accrual, the prepaid gas sector lagged as issuance surged into year-end, which caused spreads to widen.

Further insight

The market moved broadly sideways during the quarter, with a modest rally following what had been a sharp gain in September. The U.S. government shutdown drove uncertainty around data releases and interest rate expectations, while municipal bond supply and selling for tax purposes drove some retrenchment into year-end. Management continued to reposition in the 12-20-year part of the yield curve with longer call dates, where we expect the best outcomes for income and positive performance. Increased allocations have been funded primarily through sales of shorter-call bonds, which lagged the recent rally despite higher income.
